channel.title: NYT > Arts > Music
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Harry Styles Left as a Dominant Male Pop Star. He Returns to a Crowd.
item.description: In the four years since the British singer last released an album, artists like Sombr, working in similar aesthetic modes, have climbed onto the charts.
item.dc:creator: Shaad D’Souza
item.category: Kiss All the Time. Disco Occasionally. (Album)
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: ‘Theory of Flames’ Dives Down the Rabbit Hole of Paranoia
item.description: Michel van der Aa’s “Theory of Flames” originates from a question: “How can we relate to people if we don’t believe in the same reality?”
item.dc:creator: Hugh Morris and Desiré Van Den Berg
item.media:credit: Desiré van den Berg for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michel van der Aa’s film opera “Theory of Flames,” premiering at the Dutch National Opera on Friday, centers on a troubled protagonist’s descent into conspiracy theories.

item.title: Squeeze Wrote a Rock Opera Five Decades Ago. It’s Coming Out Now.
item.description: During its early days, the band came up with songs about a fictional nightclub. After a career filled with ups and downs, it finally returned to “Trixies.”
item.dc:creator: David Peisner
item.media:credit: Jono White for The New York Times
item.media:description: Chris Difford and Glenn Tilbrook of Squeeze. Their new (old) album, “Trixies,” is a fascinating window into the group’s musical roots.

item.title: On ‘You’re Free to Go,’ Anjimile Explores Rebirth and Transformation
item.description: The singer-songwriter’s latest album, “You’re Free to Go,” has poppier influences undergirding its signature gut punch.
item.dc:creator: David Peisner
item.media:credit: Sebastian Siadecki for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Living takes work,” Anjimile said. “Sometimes, it’s really hard but I feel like I have a base line now of ‘I’m going to be OK.’”

item.title: Britney Spears Is Arrested on Suspicion of D.U.I. in California
item.description: “Hopefully this can be the first step in long overdue change that needs to occur in Britney’s life,” a representative for the pop star said after she was released from jail.
item.dc:creator: Michaela Towfighi and Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Valerie Macon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Britney Spears was pulled over after officers observed her driving erratically at high speeds, a California Highway Patrol spokesman said.

item.title: Druski Talks About His Influences, Hollywood Ambitions and Timothée Chalamet
item.description: The comedian is building a galaxy of collaborators — Timothée Chalamet, Kai Cenat and Justin Bieber — and telling us how they fit into his future.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica and Joe Coscarelli
item.media:credit: Jason Nocito for The New York Times

item.title: Chadwick Boseman’s Hip-Hop Play, in Shakespeare’s House
item.description: The actor Chadwick Boseman was a playwright, too. At Shakespeare’s Globe in London, his “Deep Azure” is drawing attention to a lost talent inspired by the Bard.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Magdalena Wosinska for The New York Times
item.media:description: Boseman studied theater at Howard University before becoming an actor.

item.title: How Jonathan Groff of ‘Just in Time’ Became Broadway’s Leading Man
item.description: By transmitting his love of live performance, the “Just in Time” actor has completed his ascendance to full musical stardom.
item.dc:creator: Ben Brantley and Mohamed Sadek
item.media:credit: Mohamed Sadek for The New York Times

item.title: Former Barclays Center Executive Says Live Nation Threatened to Pull Tours
item.description: The Justice Department had John Abbamondi testify at an antitrust trial to support its case that Live Nation has acted as a monopoly, an accusation the company denies.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Vincent Alban/The New York Times
item.media:description: Barclays Center in Brooklyn was the subject of heated testimony at the Live Nation trial on Wednesday.

item.title: Bob Power, 73, Hip-Hop Engineer and Tribe Called Quest Collaborator, Dies
item.description: He helped pioneer a newly complex approach to sampling in rap music, including on A Tribe Called Quest’s influential 1991 album “The Low End Theory.”
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Cindy Ord/WireImage for The Recording Academy, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Bob Power in 2016. “Great music is made by people who either don’t care or don’t understand what is ‘normal,’ so they do something extraordinary,” he said.

item.title: John P. Hammond, Pioneer in 1960s Blues Renaissance, Dies at 83
item.description: With his acclaimed interpretations of Delta Blues standards, he was a fixture on the Greenwich Village music scene for decades.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen
item.media:credit: The Estate of Edmund Teske, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John P. Hammond in 1962. “When I first heard blues, I was completely turned on to it and it became larger than life,” he said. “And then it became my life.”

item.title: Tree Rings Reveal Origins of Some of the World’s Best Violins
item.description: A study of over 275 violins suggests that Antonio Stradivari used wood from high-altitude forests in northern Italy to craft some of his most renowned instruments.
item.dc:creator: Katherine Kornei

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Ray Barretto
item.description: A son of Puerto Rican parents who grew up in the Bronx, Barretto moved between jazz and Latin music with relentless creativity. Hear 12 indestructible tracks.
item.dc:creator: Giovanni Russonello

item.title: Live Nation Accused of Hurting Music Fans as Antitrust Trial Begins
item.description: The Justice Department lawsuit says the concert giant acts as a monopoly in the music industry, a charge the company denies.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Mike Blake/Reuters
item.media:description: The Trump Administration is pursuing a major antitrust case against Live Nation, the concert giant who government lawyers say unfairly dominates the music industry.

item.title: Choosin’ 9 Songs About Texas
item.description: Listen to Ella Langley’s Hot 100 hit and more songs about a place that’s long been a source of musical inspiration.
item.dc:creator: Lindsay Zoladz
item.media:credit: Seth Herald/Reuters

item.title: Gabriel Kreuther Fires Up Alsatian Cooking at Saverne
item.description: Eddie Huang’s Baohaus makes a return, Da Toscano moves into the Iroquois Hotel and more restaurant news.
item.dc:creator: Florence Fabricant

item.title: Klaus Mäkelä’s Talents and Faults With the Chicago Symphony
item.description: The Chicago Symphony Orchestra has placed a huge bet on Klaus Mäkelä for its future. It’s still too soon to tell whether that will pay off.
item.dc:creator: David Allen

item.title: Met Opera Gambles on Yuval Sharon for Its New ‘Tristan’
item.description: Yuval Sharon, known for his bold approach to opera, is making his Met debut with what he has called “the single hardest work” to stage.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nagourney
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times
item.media:description: A rehearsal of Wagner’s “Tristan und Isolde,” which opens at the Metropolitan Opera on March 9.

item.title: Should I Get Into Vinyl Records?
item.description: There are plenty of reasons to consider collecting and playing LPs of your favorite music. Saving money isn’t one of them.
item.dc:creator: David Renard

item.title: Two Approaches to Musical Time, From the Vienna Philharmonic
item.description: The orchestra returned to Carnegie Hall for three concerts, led by Andris Nelsons, in which the playing was inconsistent but also moving.
item.dc:creator: Corinna da Fonseca-Wollheim

item.title: ‘Gentleman Jack’ Brings a Queer Romance to Ballet
item.description: Annabelle Lopez Ochoa’s new ballet, based on the life of one of the first modern lesbians, is changing how dancers view their traditional roles.
item.dc:creator: Laura Cappelle and Sophie Stafford
item.media:credit: Sophie Stafford for The New York Times

item.title: For a Lesbian Twist on ‘Cyrano,’ They Enlisted an Indigo Girl
item.description: Opening a decade after “Fun Home” debuted on Broadway, the new musical “Starstruck” has a lesbian protagonist and a female creative team. That still seems radical.
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli
item.media:credit: Evelyn Freja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Clockwise from left, Mary Ann Stratton, Emily Saliers and Beth Malone collaborated on “Starstruck.”

item.title: Antitrust Trial to Challenge Live Nation’s Grip on the Music Industry
item.description: A jury will decide whether the music colossus, which owns venues, represents artists and operates Ticketmaster, is a monopoly, as the Justice Department contends.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario and David McCabe
item.media:credit: Allison Dinner/EPA, via Shutterstock
item.media:description: Last year, Live Nation put on 55,000 events and sold 646 million tickets around the world. The government says the company has used its power to stifle competition and drive up ticket prices.

item.title: After ‘Priceless’ Violin Flies on Lap, Lufthansa Changes Carry-On Rules
item.description: Carolin Widmann had to cradle her centuries-old, multimillion-dollar instrument during a flight to Germany. Her predicament resonated with musicians who have faced similar challenges.
item.dc:creator: Christine Hauser
item.media:credit: Brill/ullstein bild, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Carolin Widmann was boarding a plane with a Guadagnini violin, made in 1782, when she was instructed to take it out of its case.

item.title: Mitski, Raye: 9 Songs We’re Talking About This Week
item.description: Mitski ponders a breakup, Raye recalls a great heartbreak and Gnarls Barkley previews its farewell album.
item.dc:creator: Jon Pareles

item.title: Neil Sedaka: 14 Essential Songs
item.description: The singer and songwriter, who died on Friday at 86, gave voice to teenage dreams, then executed one of music’s most unexpected comebacks.
item.dc:creator: Stephen Thomas Erlewine
item.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
item.media:description: Neil Sedaka at home in California last summer, when he was playing a residency at a lounge called Vitello’s.

item.title: Neil Sedaka, Singing Craftsman of Memorable Pop Songs, Dies at 86
item.description: He sang and co-wrote some of the definitive teenage anthems of the 1950s and early ’60s, including “Breaking Up Is Hard to Do,” and then reinvented his career in the ’70s.
item.dc:creator: Peter Applebome
item.media:credit: Michael Putland/Getty Images
item.media:description: Neil Sedaka performing onstage in London in 1977. He intersected in his career with a remarkably diverse array of musicians.

item.title: Review: Harpist Parker Ramsay Merges Old and New With Friends
item.description: The multifaceted musician Parker Ramsay assembled an evening of Baroque and contemporary music, including a premiere by Georg Friedrich Haas.
item.dc:creator: Anastasia Tsioulcas

item.title: Why the Vienna Philharmonic Played Nat King Cole Hits
item.description: At a gala in New York, the orchestra recognized two of his daughters, who are underwriting a scholarship to its academy.
item.dc:creator: James Barron
item.media:credit: Dieter Nagl/Agence France-Presse, via Wiener Philharmoniker
